title: Influencer
author: Joseph Grenny, Kerry Patterson, et al.

chapters.title: Influence is a learnable skill that helps you reach lasting goals
chapters.text:
Society's toughest challenges rarely yield to another app, dataset, or academic model. What moves the needle is our ability to change how people act — a craft worth mastering whether you lead a company, raise children, or volunteer in your town. Social media makes influence look effortless, yet the best practitioners treat it as disciplined engineering, not charisma on autopilot. They obsess over three levers others overlook:

• Focus and measurement: crystal-clear outcomes paired with relentless tracking.
• Vital behaviors: the smallest set of actions that actually produce the result.
• Six sources of influence: personal motivation, personal ability, social motivation, social ability, structural motivation, and structural ability — mapped, then activated on purpose.

Here is the relief: these methods can be copied. The playbook is not reserved for celebrities. Influence shows up in hospitals, classrooms, factories, and neighborhoods whenever someone needs habits to stick. This summary distills a repeatable system for leading transformation and giving everyday people the tools to matter.

Beyond viral fame, influence is the quiet work of helping teams adopt safety habits and communities solve shared problems. Grenny and colleagues show that when you combine the six sources deliberately, change stops depending on a single heroic personality.

Top influencers refuse to begin with fuzzy ambitions. They convert the finish line into a vivid, measurable declaration everyone can rally around. ~ Joseph Grenny et al.

chapters.title: Start by defining a sharp goal and measuring it often
chapters.text:
Before you persuade anyone, nail the target and the scoreboard. Describe the future state in plain language, then instrument the journey so you can see behavior bending week by week. Initiatives die early when sponsors skip this discipline. Watch for three recurring errors:

• Vague goals
• Infrequent or absent tracking
• Measuring the wrong thing

Vague goals

Language shapes followership. A sharp objective lights up both reason and emotion. Dr. Don Berwick, former CEO of the Institute for Healthcare Improvement, stared at a grim statistic: preventable medical harm ranked as America's sixth leading killer. In December 2004 he asked the field to save 100,000 lives by June 14, 2006, at 9 a.m. The 100,000 Lives Campaign beat the number because "save lives" was unmistakable, quantified, and bound to a calendar everyone could see.

Frame goals that are specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound.

Infrequent or no measures

Clarity without cadence is decoration. Metrics influence action only when they arrive often enough to correct course while habits are still forming.

Bad measures

Frequent measurement still fails if the dial tracks the wrong activity. Spend real time selecting indicators that forecast the influence you need.

Launch every project with a hardened goal statement, then review the right signals on a rhythm people can feel. Replace guessing with evidence about whether the behaviors you care about are actually shifting.

Did you know? University of Scranton data indicates roughly 92% of New Year's resolutions never reach completion.

To be an influencer in any sphere, the first thing you need to do is to focus and measure. For a start, the goal you want to achieve needs to be highly clear, and you should look for ways to measure your progress.

Most people who desire to be influencers fail from the onset because they neglect this step. Some of the common mistakes that undermine people’s ability to influence others are: • Vague goals • Infrequent or no measures • Bad measures Vague goals The way you define your objectives determines whether you will influence others.

chapters.title: Target a handful of vital behaviors instead of sweeping overhauls
chapters.text:
With goals locked and measured, ask which human actions must change. Surprisingly often, one or two vital behaviors carry most of the weight. Trying to fix every meal, meeting, or mood at once dilutes energy. If nutrition is the aim, isolate the keystone habit instead of rewriting the entire diet overnight.

Before pouring energy into the wrong levers, discover which behavior disproportionately drives your outcome. Influencers hunt vital behaviors with four searches:
• Notice the obvious
• Look for crucial moments
• Learn from positive deviants
• Spot culture busters

Notice the obvious

Call out practices everyone already knows yet rarely uses. Meetings run daily, yet basic rules for respectful debate sit idle on shelves.

Look for crucial moments

Zoom in on pinch points — seconds when a single choice flips success to failure. Ignore the 98% of routines that already work; redesign around the 2% that trigger collapse.

Learn from positive deviants

Positive deviants outperform peers inside the same constraints. Find them, interview them, and translate their tactics into teachable steps.

Spot culture busters

Some destructive habits survive because no one challenges taboo topics. Invite candid conversation about norms that harm performance.

After the search, run a quick proof: does more of the behavior reliably lift the outcome you care about? Validate with data before you broadcast.

After making clear, compelling, and measurable goals, the next thing to do is identify behaviors — anything humans do — that need to be changed to achieve the desired result. Usually, these behaviors do not need to be numerous; one or two vital behaviors are enough.

For example, you could identify unhealthy eating habits as something you’d like to change with a secret recipe. To avoid focusing all your energy on the wrong behaviors, take the time to find the specific behavior that will be useful in achieving your goal.

chapters.title: Personal motivation and ability reshape what people will do alone
chapters.text:
Personal motivation tackles the "I don't want to" barrier. Skilled influencers help people extract satisfaction from tasks they usually avoid:

• Allow for choice: autonomy increases commitment when refusal remains legitimate.
• Create direct experiences: feeling consequences beats hearing about them in a lecture hall.
• Tell the story: when live demos are impossible, narrative can make abstract risks tangible.
• Game it: clear rules, scores, and feedback turn chores into contests people want to win.

When these tools work, compliance fades because the behavior starts to feel intrinsically rewarding.

Personal ability answers "I can't." Replace passive audiences with deliberate practice. Drill the skill, offer immediate feedback, and decompose vital behaviors into steps small enough to log daily. Design practice loops that treat mistakes as data, not shame.

Hard work pays off in the future. Laziness pays off now. ~ Steven Wright

Personal motivation means influencing others to take pleasure in what they do not particularly enjoy. Influencers have used some of these outlined techniques to help people love what they hate: • Allow for choice: the best way to get commitment from others is by letting them know that they have the power to say no.

This is an excellent way to transform people because it spurs them to adopt new and better behaviors. When creating direct experiences is difficult, using a meaningful and persuasive story can be a powerful method to influence behavior change.

chapters.title: Social motivation and ability turn peers into amplifiers of change
chapters.text:
Nothing persuades like a leader living the new standard. Speeches ring hollow when your calendar, budget, and ego still worship the old way. Show that you will surrender time, money, or pride before asking others to sacrifice. Recruit credible insiders instead of marching in alone.

When culture pushes back, make the conversation public. Name the shift and create peer accountability for reinforcing right behaviors while interrupting harmful ones.

Collaboration fuels creativity, throughput, and better collective results.

Modeling is only half the job. Social ability designs scaffolding so people help one another execute vital behaviors. Structure teams that trade knowledge, resources, and social capital. James Surowiecki shows groups routinely outperform solo heroes when incentives align.

The best way to influence others to adopt difficult or uncommon behaviors is to practice those behaviors; you can’t just tell people around the new behavior; they have to see you live by it. Generally, people won't even trust what you're saying if you're unwilling to sacrifice your old values for new ones; they need to see you surrender crucial things like money, time, and ego before following you.

As you influence others, try to seek the support of individuals who have a relationship with the people you're trying to influence. These intermediaries will help encourage those you intend to influence to take action.

Look for ways to partner with these crucial people, don't try to do it alone. As you continue your influencing efforts, you might discover that you need to make certain changes in specific ways due to existing norms.

chapters.title: Structural levers work best after personal and social forces are in place
chapters.text:
Managers love starting with bonuses, fines, or promotions. Yet carrots and sticks often corrode influence when they become the whole story. Durable change links behaviors to meaning, activates peer support, and only then adds incentives as seasoning.

Mark Lepper's 1973 experiments demonstrated that paying children for drawing they already enjoyed reduced interest once payments stopped. Rewards must target vital behaviors, not headline metrics.

Structural ability edits the physical stage. Criminologist George Kelling argued disorder broadcasts permission to break rules. Audit environments, remove triggers, and embed reminders that prompt the conduct you want.

Most leaders believe in using structural motivations like incentives, salaries, and bonuses to spur people's changes. Building a stable audience means you do not allow positive and negative incentives to undermine your ability to influence others.

In the long run, most incentives tend to backfire because people see them as the first motivation strategy to impact others. To motivate others, allow vital behaviors to connect intrinsically with people first, then create social support before thinking of incentives.

Research conducted by Dr. Mark Lepper in 1973 revealed that rewarding people to engage in an activity they already enjoy tends to yield bad results.

When the reward withdraws, people might do less of the activity; but this is not to say incentives are wrong. Still, you have to learn to use them wisely and not allow them to take the place of personal and social motivation.

Always ensure that it is the vital behaviors people are showing that you're rewarding, not the results. Incentives work better when tailored to the needs of the audience.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
You already own the ingredients for influence; the gap is applying the full recipe with patience. Declare outcomes plainly, measure them often, and insist on indicators tied to behavior. Resist fixing twenty habits; isolate the few that unlock everything else.

At work, influence is how teams hit strategic targets and how trust compounds. Stay transparent when reality wobbles — explain intent instead of performing perfection.

Try this

If your block feels anonymous, be the first neighbor who says hello and offers help.

Read it till the end?

You have all it takes to influence the behavior of others; just use the right tools and be willing to put in the necessary effort. To become an effective influencer in any niche, you must learn to focus and measure.

First, state clearly what you want to achieve, then measure your goals frequently to track your progress. Also, ensure that you track the right things and focus on a specific behavior.

It’s essential to take your time to identify what you want, don't rush things. Don't waste your time searching for numerous behaviors that will point you in the wrong direction.

Instead, focus on a few vital behaviors that will bring around your desired result. Lastly, as you create your influence strategy, use all the sources of influence: personal motivation and ability, social motivation, and structural motivation.

Impact is crucial in the workplace because it's the best way to help others achieve crucial company objectives. Influencing others helps you build strong relationships based on the trust your audience has in your methods.
